Animals and plants exhibit a wide range of movements. Streaming of protoplasm in the unicellular organisms like Amoeba is a simple form of movement. Movement of cilia, flagella and tentacles are... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: LOCOMOTION AND MOVEMENT 217, 17.1 TYPES OF MOVEMENT, LOCOMOTION AND MOVEMENT, CHAPTER 17. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Breathing and Exchange of Gases is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that lOCOMOTION AND MOVEMENT 217 Movement is one of the significant features of living beings.

The text explains that animals and plants exhibit a wide range of movements.

The text explains that streaming of protoplasm in the unicellular organisms like Amoeba is a simple form of movement.

The text explains that movement of cilia, flagella and tentacles are shown by many organisms.

The text explains that human beings can move limbs, jaws, eyelids, tongue, etc.

The text explains that some of the movements result in a change of place or location.

The text explains that such voluntary movements are called locomotion.

The text explains that walking, running, climbing, flying and swimming are all some forms of locomotory movements.

The text explains that locomotory structures need not be different from those affecting other types of movements.

The text explains that for example, in Paramoecium, cilia helps in the movement of food through cytopharynx and in locomotion as well.

The text explains that hydra can use its tentacles for capturing its prey and also use them for locomotion.

The text explains that we use limbs for changes in body postures and locomotion as well.

The text explains that the above observations suggest that movements and locomotion cannot be studied separately.

The text explains that the two may be linked by stating that all locomotions are movements but all movements are not locomotions.

The text explains that methods of locomotion performed by animals vary with their habitats and the demand of the situation.

The text explains that however, locomotion is generally for search of food, shelter, mate, suitable breeding grounds, favourable climatic conditions or to escape from enemies/predators.

The text explains that lOCOMOTION AND MOVEMENT CHAPTER 17 17.1 Types of Movement 17.2 Muscle 17.3 Skeletal System 17.4 Joints 17.5 Disorders of Muscular and Skeletal System

The text explains that locomotion requires a perfect coordinated activity of muscular, skeletal and neural systems.

The text explains that in this chapter, you will learn about the types of muscles, their structure, mechanism of their contraction and important aspects of the skeletal system.

The text explains that 17.2 MUSCLE You have studied in Chapter 8 that the cilia and flagella are the outgrowths of the cell membrane.

The text explains that flagellar movement helps in the swimming of spermatozoa, maintenance of water current in the canal system of sponges and in locomotion of Protists like Euglena.

The text explains that as their activities are under the voluntary control of the nervous system, they are known as voluntary muscles too.

The text explains that their activities are not under the voluntary control of the nervous system and are therefore known as involuntary muscles.

The text explains that a characteristic feature of the muscle fibre is the presence of a large number of parallelly arranged filaments in the sarcoplasm called myofilaments or myofibrils.

The text explains that they are involuntary in nature as the nervous system does not control their activities directly.

The text explains that let us examine a skeletal muscle in detail to understand the structure and mechanism of contraction.

The text explains that both the proteins are arranged as rod-like structures, parallel to each other and also to the longitudinal axis of the myofibrils.

The text explains that lOCOMOTION AND MOVEMENT 221 17.2.1 Structure of Contractile Proteins Each actin (thin) filament is made of two ‘F’ (filamentous) actins helically wound to each other.

The text explains that figure 17.3 (a) An actin (thin) filament (b) Myosin monomer (Meromyosin) Actin binding sites ATP binding sites Head Cross arm (a) (b) 17.2.2 Mechanism of Muscle Contraction Mechanism of muscle contraction is best explained by the sliding filament theory which states that contraction of a muscle fibre takes place by the sliding of the thin filaments over the thick filaments.

The text explains that 222 222 222 222 222 BIOLOGY Muscle contraction is initiated by a signal sent by the central nervous system (CNS) via a motor neuron.

The text explains that the process continues till the Ca++ ions are pumped back to the sarcoplasmic cisternae resulting in the masking of actin filaments.

The text explains that 224 BIOLOGY 17.3 SKELETAL SYSTEM Skeletal system consists of a framework of bones and a few cartilages.

The text explains that this system has a significant role in movement shown by the body.

The text explains that in human beings, this system is made up of 206 bones and a few cartilages.

The text explains that the 8th, 9th and 10th pairs of ribs do not articulate directly with the sternum but join the seventh rib with the help of hyaline cartilage.

The text explains that the bones of the hand (fore limb) are Cervical vertebra Intervertebral disc Sacrum Coccyx Thoracic vertebra Lumbar vertebra Figure 17.7 Vertebral column (right lateral view) Figure 17.8 Ribs and rib cage

The text explains that 226 BIOLOGY Figure 17.9 Right pectoral girdle and upper arm.

The text explains that the dorsal, flat, triangular body of scapula has a slightly elevated ridge called the spine which projects as a flat, expanded process called the acromion.

The text explains that locomotory movements are no exception to Figure 17.10 Right pelvic girdle and lower limb bones (frontal view)

The text explains that 17.5 DISORDERS OF MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL SYSTEM Myasthenia gravis: Auto immune disorder affecting neuromuscular junction leading to fatigue, weakening and paralysis of skeletal muscle.

The text explains that sUMMARY Movement is an essential feature of all living beings.

The text explains that bones and cartilages constitute our skeletal system.

The text explains that the skeletal system is divisible into axial and appendicular.

The text explains that (e) Sternum is present on the ventral side of the body.

The text explains that write the difference between : (a) Actin and Myosin (b) Red and White muscles (c) Pectoral and Pelvic girdle 6.

The text explains that match Column I with Column II : Column I Column II (a) Smooth muscle (i) Myoglobin (b) Tropomyosin (ii) Thin filament (c) Red muscle (iii) Sutures (d) Skull (iv) Involuntary 7.

The text explains that what are the different types of movements exhibited by the cells of human body?

The text explains that how do you distinguish between a skeletal muscle and a cardiac muscle?

The text explains that name the type of joint between the following:- (a) atlas/axis (b) carpal/metacarpal of thumb (c) between phalanges (d) femur/acetabulum (e) between cranial bones (f) between pubic bones in the pelvic girdle 10.

The text explains that fill in the blank spaces: (a) All mammals (except a few) have __________ cervical vertebra.

The text explains that (b) The number of phalanges in each limb of human is __________ (c) Thin filament of myofibril contains 2 ‘F’ actins and two other proteins namely __________ and __________.

The text explains that (d) In a muscle fibre Ca++ is stored in __________ (e) __________ and __________ pairs of ribs are called floating ribs.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 17: Breathing and Exchange of Gases
